Re
Fnrn Polrrrcel PRACTrcps C oMMrssroN
.128 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramenro, CA 95811-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) -122-0886
March 26,2009
Aaron P. Noble
Department of General Services
Division of the State Architect
1102 Q Street
Sacramento, California 958I I
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09-059
Dear Mr. Noble:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Please note that our advice is based solely
on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of othei
conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest, Govemment Code Section
1090 or Public Contract Code Section 10410.
Please also note that Govemment Code Section Lggg}, which is not part of the Act, may
be applicable to your situation. Generally, Government Code Section 1999d allows each state
agency to develop a statement of incompatible activities, which includes specific enterprises or
employment "clearly inconsistent, incompatible, in conflict with, or inimical to" the duties of the
agency's officials and employees. As we do not offer advice beyond the confines of the Act, we
must refer you to your agency's counsel and statement of incompatible activities to ensure that
your activities do not violate conflict-of-interest laws outside the Act or are not otherwise
incompatible with your responsibilities to the agency.
QUESTTON
As a senior architect at the Division of State Architect whose duties include
developing proposed changes to the California Building Code for adoption by the California
Building Standards Commission, may you conduct private seminars for architects relating to
existing provisions of the California Building Code?
' Th. Poli,i.al Ref<-rrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 1000 throug h glol4. All statutory
ret'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fiir political practises
Commission are contained in Sections I tt I l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of thi Calitbrnia Code otrRegulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. ,4.-09-059
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions do not preclude a public official from concurrent
private-sector employment. However, operating a private business while alio being a public
official may, under certain circumstancei, give iis" to a conflict of interest that disqualifies the
official from governmerrtal decisionmaking. The answer to your question depends on whether
the Building Code dccisions in which you are involved will have a reasonabllioreseeable
material financial effect on your students, who would be economic interests to yo, as sources of
income. The students would be economic interest to you if they pay you $500 lr more within
12 months prior to your participation in a governmental decision. ti itrese students will not pay
you $500 or more in any l2-month period, as you have indicated, they would not be sources of
income to you and any impact on your studenti of govemmental deciiions in which you
participate would not give rise to a conflict or interest.
FACTS
You are employed as a senior architect by the Department of General Services, Division
of the state Architect ("DSA"). Your duties at IiSA include developing f.opor"o building code
changes pertaining to accessibility for persons with disabilities. Some Jr trr"r" proposed changes
are ultimately submitted to the Building Standards Commission for adoption. Thebuilding 
e
Standards Commission (the "Commission") is an independent commission within the State and
Consumer Services Agency. Its commissioners are appointed by the Governor.
Changes to the California Building Code (the "Building Code") may be proposed by
DSA staff or by members of the general public for DSA review. The Commission has an annual
"code cycle" where recommended accessibility code changes can be submitted by DSA for
consideration by the-commission. In your poiition, you riuk" recommendations to DSA.as to
what code changes should go before the Commission. You also appear before the commission,
or the Commission's uc""siibility advisory committee, to present DSA proposals for changing
the Building Code. From time to time, you also serve as a technicul ."pies"ntative to DSA
public committees that advise the State Architect regarding proposed Building code changes.
In our telephone conversation of March 13,20Og you stated that DSA is statutorily
prohibited from enhancing or diminishing accessibility requirements in the Building Code except
as necessary to meet federal minimum accessibility standards of the federal Americans with
Disabilities Act of 1990, citing Government code section4459(a).
As part of your own private business, you are contemplating providing seminars for
architects regarding existing accessibility provisions in the nuitaing bode. yiu intend to offer
these seminars primarily to Califomia licensed architects. ln our telephone conversation of
March 24, 2009, you indicated that in no event would any of your stuients Ue faying $500 or
more in any l2-month period for attending your seminari. You would teach during your off-
duty time and would include in all seminar materials a statement that the state does not endorse,
sponsor or approve the seminar.


File No. A-09-059
Page No. 3
In our telephone conversation you also indicated that changes to the Building Code,s
accessibility requirements may affect your students or their busine-sses, but only miriimally. The
thanges would place more stringent requirements pertaining to accessibility for p"iron, ,^iiifr^"'
disabilities. These requirements would apply to eiisting buitdings undergoing alteration. The
revised building standards would minimally increase an-architect's work-on a prolect and would
also cause an increase in the costs of alterations. Whether an architect is paid on an hourly basis
or as a percentage of the cost of a building project, the changes that would make accessibiiity
standards more stringent could increase thgrevenues or income of some of your students.
ANALYSIS
As stated in our prior letter dated February 5,2009, Section 87100 of the Act prohibits
any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in whichlhe official has a financial interest. The
FPPC has adopted an eighrstep standard analysis for deciding whether an official has a
disqualifying confl ict of interest. (Regulation i 8ZOO(U).)
Step 1. Are you a "public official" within the meaning of Section g7100?
Section 82048 defines a publicofficial as "every member, officer, employee or consultant
of a state or local govemment agency." As a senior architect at DSA, which is a state
govemment agency, you are a public official Therefore, you may not make, participate in
making, or otherwise use your position to influence any decisionj that will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on any of your economic interests.
Step 2' Will you be making, participating in making or inlluencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her o-ffi." or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency' (Regulation _18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision,, when,
acting within the authority of his or her position urd *ithout signiflcant substantive or
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the
decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulat ion tg702.2.) A public official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influenc" i gor"rr-ental decision which is before
his or her agency for the purposes of influencing the decisiln, the official contacts or appears
before or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee, or consulta nt of his or
her agency. (Regulation 18702.3(a).) A pubiic official is attemptin-g to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision which is before an agency other than the fficial,s


File No. 4-09-059
Page No.4
?8:"9! if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official acts or purports to act, on
behalf of, or as rhe representative of his or her agency. (Regulation rszo).rtu).1
You have described two kinds of decisions in which you are involved as a public official.
The first is DSA's decision whether to present certain propoied Building Code changes to the
Commission. The second is the Commission's decision whether to aaoit rh"r";;;; I --
When you make recommendations to DSA as to which Building Code changes it should
present to the Commission for adoption, you are participating inDSAi decision whether to
recommend these changes to the commission. Regulation tgloz.z(b)(2) provides that a public
official participates in making a governmental dec[ion when the official advises or makes
recommendations to the decisionmaker by "preparing or presenting any report, analysis, or
opinion, orally, or inwriting, wfrich requiresthJ exercise-ofjudgm-ent;" th";; of the official
and the purpose of which is to influence a governmental decision referenced i1 fitt" 2, California
Code of Regulations, section I87OI(a)(2XA)."
When you appear before the Commission, which is an agency other than your agency, to
present DSA- recommended changes for adoption, you are attemptt;g to use your official
position to influence the decisions of the Commission.
Step 3. What are your economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from certain enumerated economic inierests. Theii economic interests are described in Section
87 103 and Regulations I 8703- I 8703.5, inclusive:
o 
{ public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of g2,000 or more. (Section g7103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a).)
o 
{ public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(d); Regulation 1 g703. l(b).)
:. 
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section SZl0gtb); ii.egulation 15703.2.)
' ll o{ telephone conversation, you implied that DSA and the Commission do not engage in
decisionmaking because they are statutorily prohibited from enhancing accessibility requiremenrs except as
necessary to meet federal standards. You stated that, as a practical rnuIt r, builders are already subject to the federal
standards. However, the statute you refer to, Government Code Section 4459(a\,merely limits the authority of DSA
and the commission, just as the authority of almost all government agencies' decisionmaking i, li.it.o in some
manner' The limitations do not mean that the agency his no decisiorimaking authority.


File No. 4-09-059
Page No. 5
o 
An official has an economic. interest in any source of income, including promised
income, totaling $500 or more within l2 months prior to the decision. (Sectioi SZtOf (");
Regulation 18703.3.)
o 
{ public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts total $420 0r more within 12 months prior to the decisio-n. (Section g7103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)
o 
d public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is commonly 
'
referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 1g703.i.)
You have an economic interest in your seminar business both as an investment (if you
invested $2,000 or more in the business) under Section 87103(a) and Regulation lg703.l(a) and
as a manager of the business entity under Section 87103(d) and Regulati6n 1g703.1(b).
Both the business entity and any clients from whom you receive income totaling $500 or
more within 12 months prior to your participating in or attempting to influence a governmental
decision will be a source of income to you. (Seciion 87103(c); R6gulation f SZ03--l-.jt^y;;;;;"
indicated that no student woutrl be paying you $500 or more in any l2_month period. Under
these circumstances, your students would not be a source of income to you *a., Section
87103(c). We caution you, however, to be mindful of the provisions of Section g7103(c) and
Regulation 18703.3(c) if- t.hrl fact changes in the future. For purposes of informing you of the
Act's requirements should that become the case, we continueoui analysis assuming that your
students become sources of income to you.
- 
Finally, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal
finances under Section 87103 and Regulation 1g703.5.
Step 4' Will your economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in decisions
you will make, participate in making or influence as rpublic official?
Under Regulation 187o4'l(a) a person, including business entities and sources of income,
is directly involved in a decision before an official'r ugJn"y when that person, either directly or
by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
t Your' employer, the Department of General services, is not considered a source of income because the
Act's definition of "income" excludes salary, reimbursement for expenses, social security, disability and other
similar benefit payments from, among otheis, a state agency. (Section g2030.)


File No. 4-09-059
Page No.6
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's-agency
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person."
a Arguably, you initiate DSA proceedings ro decide which Building Code changes will be recommended ro
the Commission' However, the process for making these decisions does not appear to require the filing of any
"application, claim, appeal or similar request.,,
5 Those students who own an architectural firm would have a financial interest in their business. This
standard would then apply to their businesses.
From the facts you have provided, you and your students will be not be initiating DSA
proceedings to recommend adoption of Building Code changes.a Similarly, you and you,
students will not be initiating commission prociedings in *hi.h decisions will be made whether
to adopt these changel. The proceedings flow from G Commission's annual ..code cycle,, where
recommended accessibility code changes can be submitted to the Commission. Nor *ill you o,
your students be named parties in, or the subjects of, these proceedings. Accordingly, you and
your students are indirectly involved in the Commission's decision.. -a business entity or source
of income that is not directly involved in a governmental decision is considered indirectly
involved. (Regulation I 8704(b).)
Personal Finances
A public official or his or her immediate family is deemed to be directly involved in a
govemmental decision that has any financial effect on his or her personal finances or those of his
or her immediate family.
Step 5. What is the applicable materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's 
"coromic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a)') Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the economic interest
and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involveJin ttre agency,s decision.
Regulation 18705.1(c) sets forth materiality standards for economic interests in business
entities that are indirectly involved in a govemmental decision, including those that are sources
of income who are also business entities. (See Regulation 18705.3(bXl): These are the standards
that would apply to your business and to your rtuJ"rts who own business entities. i -F;;*^**-"
relatively small businesses' the financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity


File No. A-09-059
Page No. 7
is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will increase/decrease its
annual gross revenues by $20,000 or more, result in the business entity incurring/avoiding
additional expenses or reducing/eliminating existing expenses by $s,ooo or more in a fiscal year
or increase/decrease the value of the business entity;s assets uy $zo,ooo or more. (Regulation
1870s.1(c)(a).)
For your students who are individuals, the effect of a decision is material if the decision
will affect the individual's income, investments, or other tangible assets or liabilities (other than
real property) by $1,000 or more. (Regulation 18705.3(b)(3)aA).)
Personal Finances
A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's or his or her immediate
family's personal finances is material if it is at least $iso in any l2-month period. (Regulation
18705.5(a).)
Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decisions on your economic interests will meet the applicarte materiariiy ltarraaraf
An effect is considered "reSsonably foreseeable" if the effect is ..substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC ops. l9g.) A financial effect need not
be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the othe; hand, if an effect is only a
mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
You have advised that any Building Code changes that are adopted by the commission
will not affect your business because no matter what thi Building codf requires, you would only
be teaching the existing Building Code provisions.
with respect to your students, you indicated in our telephone conversation that changes to
the Building Code's accessibility requirements may affect some of them or their businesses, but
only minimally. You stated that theJe amendmenti to the Building Code would create more
stringent requirements pertaining to accessibility for persons with disabilities. These
requirements would Ppl{ to existing build.ings undergoing alteration. The revised building
standards would minimally increase an architect's woik oi a proiect and would also cause an
increase in the costs of alterations. whether an architect is paid on un hourly Luri, o, u, u
percentage of the cost-of a building project, it is reasonablyioreseeable that a decision to change
the Building code under these circumsiances would increase the architect's revenues or income.
Applying Step 5 to those students who may become sources of income to you as
described in Step 3 and who own businesses, for a relatively small business, the financial effect
of a govemmental decision on the business entity is material if it is ,"uroruuty foreseeable that
the governmental decision will increase/de"r.u.i its annual gross revenues by $20,000 or more.
For students who do not own businesses, the effect of a decision is material if the decision will
affect the individual's income by $1,000 or more. (Regulation 1g705.3(b)(3XA).)


File No. 4-09-059
Page No. 8
To determine if the financial effect of a governmental decision making accessibility
standards more stringent.is "material," the archiiects who attend your classes"would need to
apply the standards set forth in Step 5 to their particular situations. We do not have facts to
enable us to make this determination.6
Steps 7 and 8. The "public generally" and "legally required participation,
exceptions
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably
foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the govemmental
decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the
public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) and l}7o7(a)),or if the official is
legally required to participate (section 87103; Regulation 18708). you have not presented any
facts indicating that either of these exceptions is applicable to your situation.
Incompatible Activities
As stated above, Government Code section lgggo,which is not part of the Act, allows each state
agency to develop a statement of incompatible activities, which includes specific enterprises or
employment "clearly inconsistent, incompatible, in conflict with, or inimical to,, the duties of the
agency's officials and employees. Because we do not offer advice beyond the confines of the Act, we
must refer you to your agency's counsel and statement of incompatibie activities to ensure that your
activities do not violate conflict-of-interest laws outside the Act o, ar" not otherwise incompatible with
your responsibilities to the agency.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
vJjgl
By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division
. 
6 However, you may conclude, as stated_above, that any financial effect of these decisions upon your
students is not "reasonably foreseeable" because the financial efiect is only a possibility, in *trictr care there is no
conflict of interest.

finances under Section 87103 and Regulation 1g703.5.
Step 4' Will your economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in decisions
you will make, participate in making or influence as rpublic official?
Under Regulation 187o4'l(a) a person, including business entities and sources of income,
is directly involved in a decision before an official'r ugJn"y when that person, either directly or
by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
t Your' employer, the Department of General services, is not considered a source of income because the
Act's definition of "income" excludes salary, reimbursement for expenses, social security, disability and other
similar benefit payments from, among otheis, a state agency. (Section g2030.)
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's-agency
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person."
a Arguably, you initiate DSA proceedings ro decide which Building Code changes will be recommended ro
the Commission' However, the process for making these decisions does not appear to require the filing of any
"application, claim, appeal or similar request.,,
5 Those students who own an architectural firm would have a financial interest in their business. This
standard would then apply to their businesses.
From the facts you have provided, you and your students will be not be initiating DSA
proceedings to recommend adoption of Building Code changes.a Similarly, you and you,
students will not be initiating commission prociedings in *hi.h decisions will be made whether
to adopt these changel. The proceedings flow from G Commission's annual ..code cycle,, where
recommended accessibility code changes can be submitted to the Commission. Nor *ill you o,
your students be named parties in, or the subjects of, these proceedings. Accordingly, you and
your students are indirectly involved in the Commission's decision.. -a business entity or source
of income that is not directly involved in a governmental decision is considered indirectly
involved. (Regulation I 8704(b).)
Personal Finances
A public official or his or her immediate family is deemed to be directly involved in a
govemmental decision that has any financial effect on his or her personal finances or those of his
or her immediate family.
Step 5. What is the applicable materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's 
"coromic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a)') Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the economic interest
and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involveJin ttre agency,s decision.
Regulation 18705.1(c) sets forth materiality standards for economic interests in business
entities that are indirectly involved in a govemmental decision, including those that are sources
of income who are also business entities. (See Regulation 18705.3(bXl): These are the standards
that would apply to your business and to your rtuJ"rts who own business entities. i -F;;*^**-"
relatively small businesses' the financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity
is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will increase/decrease its
annual gross revenues by $20,000 or more, result in the business entity incurring/avoiding
additional expenses or reducing/eliminating existing expenses by $s,ooo or more in a fiscal year
or increase/decrease the value of the business entity;s assets uy $zo,ooo or more. (Regulation
1870s.1(c)(a).)
For your students who are individuals, the effect of a decision is material if the decision
will affect the individual's income, investments, or other tangible assets or liabilities (other than
real property) by $1,000 or more. (Regulation 18705.3(b)(3)aA).)
Personal Finances
A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's or his or her immediate
family's personal finances is material if it is at least $iso in any l2-month period. (Regulation
18705.5(a).)
Step 6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decisions on your economic interests will meet the applicarte materiariiy ltarraaraf
An effect is considered "reSsonably foreseeable" if the effect is ..substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC ops. l9g.) A financial effect need not
be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the othe; hand, if an effect is only a
mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
You have advised that any Building Code changes that are adopted by the commission
will not affect your business because no matter what thi Building codf requires, you would only
be teaching the existing Building Code provisions.
with respect to your students, you indicated in our telephone conversation that changes to
the Building Code's accessibility requirements may affect some of them or their businesses, but
only minimally. You stated that theJe amendmenti to the Building Code would create more
stringent requirements pertaining to accessibility for persons with disabilities. These
requirements would Ppl{ to existing build.ings undergoing alteration. The revised building
standards would minimally increase an architect's woik oi a proiect and would also cause an
increase in the costs of alterations. whether an architect is paid on un hourly Luri, o, u, u
percentage of the cost-of a building project, it is reasonablyioreseeable that a decision to change
the Building code under these circumsiances would increase the architect's revenues or income.
Applying Step 5 to those students who may become sources of income to you as
described in Step 3 and who own businesses, for a relatively small business, the financial effect
of a govemmental decision on the business entity is material if it is ,"uroruuty foreseeable that
the governmental decision will increase/de"r.u.i its annual gross revenues by $20,000 or more.
For students who do not own businesses, the effect of a decision is material if the decision will
affect the individual's income by $1,000 or more. (Regulation 1g705.3(b)(3XA).)
To determine if the financial effect of a governmental decision making accessibility
standards more stringent.is "material," the archiiects who attend your classes"would need to
apply the standards set forth in Step 5 to their particular situations. We do not have facts to
enable us to make this determination.6
Steps 7 and 8. The "public generally" and "legally required participation,
exceptions
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably
foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the govemmental
decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the
public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) and l}7o7(a)),or if the official is
legally required to participate (section 87103; Regulation 18708). you have not presented any
facts indicating that either of these exceptions is applicable to your situation.
Incompatible Activities
As stated above, Government Code section lgggo,which is not part of the Act, allows each state
agency to develop a statement of incompatible activities, which includes specific enterprises or
employment "clearly inconsistent, incompatible, in conflict with, or inimical to,, the duties of the
agency's officials and employees. Because we do not offer advice beyond the confines of the Act, we
must refer you to your agency's counsel and statement of incompatibie activities to ensure that your
activities do not violate conflict-of-interest laws outside the Act o, ar" not otherwise incompatible with
your responsibilities to the agency.
